name: lentil mushroom stew
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS ON THE SAME BASE. Sauté the soffrito (yellow onion, celery, carrots) in olive oil. Add pureed portabella mushrooms and cook them down. Fold in finely chopped potatoes and lentils (both go in at the same time — see notes). The rest of the build forks by variant.

=== VARIANT A — FRENCH (WINE + ROSEMARY + HERBES DE PROVENCE + LIGHT TOMATO) ===

A french provincial direction — the wine + herb axis leads. Tomato is present but minimal (structural buddy, not headliner).

1. Add half or one whole finely chopped tomato — light, structural. Cook it down briefly.
2. Deglaze with a generous splash of WHITE WINE — this is the biggest flavor builder in this variant. Let it reduce.
3. Add rosemary and herbes de provence. Season with black pepper and salt.
4. Add stock to cover. Slow cook covered for a long time.
5. If the broth is too thin at the end, stir in a starch slurry.
6. NO tomato paste in variant A — it fights the wine.

=== VARIANT B — ITALIAN (DEEP TOMATO + BAY LEAF, NO WINE) ===

A mediterranean tomato direction — the tomato axis leads. No wine.

1. Add one whole finely chopped tomato AND a spoonful of tomato paste. Cook the tomato paste hard into the oil until it darkens a shade.
2. Add bay leaf. Add oregano; optionally fennel seeds (crushed) or fennel powder for a more ratatouille-adjacent lean. Black pepper and salt.
3. Add stock — but only as much as you need for coverage. In variant B, less stock means more tomato-concentrated broth; more stock reads as a lighter minestrone-adjacent stew. Choose based on how tomato-deep you want to go.
4. Slow cook covered for a long time.
5. If the broth is too thin, starch slurry finish.
6. NO wine in variant B — it fights the tomato.
notes:
TWO AXES, THEY FIGHT A LITTLE — PICK ONE:
- FRENCH AXIS (Variant A): wine + rosemary + herbes de provence carry the flavor. Tomato is present in a supporting role (half or one whole fresh tomato) but never dominant.
- ITALIAN / MEDITERRANEAN AXIS (Variant B): tomato + tomato paste + bay leaf + italian seasonings (see italian minestrone soup and ratatouille for the seasoning language). No wine.
The two axes can technically collaborate in small doses, but wine and deep tomato fight each other — the acid registers clash and the dish reads muddled. Lean hard into one direction; don't try to hedge. Stock is the shared glue for both variants — use as much stock as you need for the broth logic on the day.

WHY THIS BASE WORKS FOR BOTH DIRECTIONS — POTATOES + PUREED MUSHROOMS ARE THE 'MEAT': what makes this recipe amenable to both the french and italian directions is that the pureed mushrooms + finely chopped potatoes fill the 'meat' slot together. In a bolognese or lentil chili, the meat is the meat; here, the mushroom-and-potato combo carries the protein-and-body role. Because that role isn't itself french or italian, the same base can go either way with the seasoning axis. This is what distinguishes lentil mushroom stew from italian minestrone (which is committed to the italian tomato path and can't pivot to french) and from french chicken soup (which is committed to the wine-and-herb path).

POTATOES ARE NON-OPTIONAL — FINELY CHOPPED, ADDED WITH THE LENTILS: potatoes are load-bearing in both variants, not an optional add-on. Chop them FINELY (small dice — not large chunks) so they cook at roughly the same rate as the lentils, and add them at the same time as the lentils. The potatoes contribute two things the dish needs: (1) starchy body that thickens the broth structurally as it simmers, replacing much of the need for the starch slurry finish, and (2) a starchy carb texture in each bite that softens the otherwise-monolithic lentil-and-mushroom mouthfeel. Without potatoes the stew reads thin and one-textured. Finely chopping — not chunk-cutting — matters because you want the potato distributed through every spoonful.

PUREED MUSHROOMS — NOT CHOPPED: portabella mushrooms should be pureed (food processor or blender) before adding, then cooked down into the base. Pureeing gives them a dense meaty backbone that reads as protein-and-body rather than as visible mushroom pieces. This matches the potato-as-carb move — both structural ingredients dissolve into the broth and support it structurally rather than showing up as discrete chunks. Chopped mushrooms give you a different (chunkier, less integrated) dish.

=== VARIANT A NOTES (FRENCH) ===

WINE IS LOAD-BEARING IN VARIANT A: a real white wine deglaze is the biggest flavor builder here. The acid and depth it adds are what lifts the dish above a generic lentil stew. Don't substitute vinegar. Don't add tomato paste on top (see the two-axes note above).

TOMATO IS LIGHT IN VARIANT A — HALF OR ONE WHOLE TOMATO ONLY: the fresh tomato in variant A is a structural buddy for the mushroom, not a flavor headliner. Half a tomato or one small whole tomato, finely chopped. Pureeing the tomato or adding more pulls the dish toward variant B territory, which is a different recipe entirely.

ROSEMARY + HERBES DE PROVENCE ARE THE HERB STACK: rosemary provides the piney second dimension against the mushroom-and-lentil base; herbes de provence rounds it with sage/lavender/thyme-in-the-mix character. Together they carry the french direction. Use rosemary lightly (at full quantity it dominates); herbes de provence more generously.

=== VARIANT B NOTES (ITALIAN) ===

DEEP TOMATO CARRIES THIS VARIANT: whole fresh tomato + tomato paste (cooked hard into the oil until darkened) is the load-bearing move. The concentration of the paste plus the fresh tomato pulp gives you the tomato-forward broth that variant B is built around.

BAY LEAF INSTEAD OF ROSEMARY: rosemary and herbes de provence pull toward french; bay leaf sits in the italian/mediterranean register and doesn't push a direction. Whole bay leaves in the simmer, fished out before serving.

ITALIAN SEASONING CHOICES — SEE MINESTRONE / RATATOUILLE FOR LANGUAGE: dried oregano is the reliable baseline. Fennel seeds (crushed in a mortar) or fennel powder add a ratatouille-adjacent lean. Both are optional; oregano alone works. Don't reach for basil or parsley here — they're finishing herbs for other italian dishes, not simmer herbs for a stew.

STOCK AS GLUE — HOW MUCH DEPENDS ON HOW TOMATO-DEEP YOU WANT: variant B is flexible on stock volume. Less stock = more tomato-concentrated broth (denser, more like an italian ragu). More stock = lighter minestrone-adjacent stew. Pick based on the day's mood. Stock is doing broth-logic here, not flavor-building; the tomato and paste are the flavor.

=== SHARED / CROSS-VARIANT NOTES ===

NO CANNED TOMATOES OR TOMATO PASTE IN VARIANT A: canned tomatoes push both variants (but especially A) toward a wrong tomato register; they're clashing across the board. Tomato paste is CLASHING in variant A but PRIMARY in variant B — it's variant-conditional. See DEEP TOMATO CARRIES THIS VARIANT above.

NO THYME: thyme is inappropriate here in either variant. The dish is mushroom-and-lentil dominant — a relatively monolithic savory profile — so it wants rosemary (variant A) or oregano (variant B) rather than thyme. Herbes de provence contains a little thyme in its blend, which is fine, but standalone dried thyme is wrong. See the herbs and greens article on the thyme-vs-rosemary axis.

NO OLD BAY: old bay belongs in italian-minestrone-soup-shaped dishes that explicitly choose it as one of the three flavoring directions. Neither variant of this dish wants the old bay axis on top; each has its own flavor backbone.

STARCH FINISH (OPTIONAL): if the broth ends up thinner than you want, stir in a small starch slurry at the end. Not always needed — the pureed mushrooms, potatoes, and lentils usually carry enough body — but it's the right move when the soup is too brothy.

STOCK IS PRIMARY HERE (NOT BOUILLON CUBES): this dish wants a real stock as the liquid, not bouillon cubes. The mushroom-and-lentil profile benefits from stock's gentler, more layered body — bouillon cubes are too concentrated and salty. Vegetable stock is the reliable default; beef stock or chicken stock could work but change the register.
